clean_story:
Hard, shiny solids. Atoms locked in grids.

The word metal comes from Greek metallon, meaning "mine" or "quarry"—because humans first found metals buried in rock, not grown like plants or born like animals. Most metals are hidden: iron rusts into red dust, gold glints in riverbeds, and aluminum hides in clay until electricity pries it loose.

Metals conduct heat and electricity like a crowd passing a bucket of water—each atom hands off energy to its neighbor without spilling a drop. Copper wires hum with electrons moving at near light-speed; steel beams hold skyscrapers upright because their atomic grids resist bending like a lattice of unbreakable Legos. A single gram of titanium, stretched thin, could wrap around Earth’s equator twice.

Yet metals tire. Bend a paperclip back and forth: the atoms slide into misaligned stacks, then snap like a zipper giving way. This is fatigue—the reason airplane wings are X-rayed for invisible cracks.

Three related subjects are alloy recipes, electroplating tricks, and how stars forge metals.
